title: Strings
description: Strings through a DSA lens: treating a string as an indexable array of characters, palindrome checks, and anagram detection.
objectives: Treat a string as an array of characters for algorithmic purposes
Solve the palindrome check problem using two pointers
Solve the anagram check problem using frequency counting
Recognize which array techniques transfer directly to strings

sections.text: std::string covered length(), substr(), find(), and concatenation. For DSA purposes, a string is best thought of the same way as an array: an indexable, iterable sequence — and nearly every array technique we've covered applies directly.
2. A String Is Just a Character Array Here

Because of this, Two Pointers, Sliding Window, and Prefix Sum all apply to strings the exact same way they apply to a vector<int> — the element type is char instead of int, but the patterns are identical.
3. Classic Pattern: Palindrome Check with Two Pointers

This is exactly Two Pointers' opposite-ends pattern: start from both ends, compare, and move inward. If any pair of matching positions disagrees, it's not a palindrome — if the pointers meet without disagreement, it is. O(n) time, O(1) extra space.
4. Classic Pattern: Anagram Check with Frequency Count

This is unordered_map's frequency-counting pattern from earlier, applied twice: count up every character in a, then count down every character in b. If both strings are true anagrams, every count lands back at exactly 0 — any leftover non-zero count means the character frequencies didn't match.

sections.title: Checking if two Scrabble racks have the exact same letters
sections.text: Comparing two Scrabble letter racks for the exact same tiles — regardless of order — is exactly the anagram-check pattern: tally what's in the first rack, subtract what's in the second, and see if everything cancels to zero. A palindrome, meanwhile, is like checking if a word reads the same from both ends inward at once — the two-pointer pattern, letter by letter.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to check string lengths first in an anagram check — different lengths can never be anagrams, and skipping this check can waste work or even cause subtle bugs.
Assuming a palindrome check needs to build a reversed copy of the string — the two-pointer approach does it in O(1) extra space, no copy needed.
Forgetting string indexing is zero-based, exactly like array indexing, when writing two-pointer or sliding-window logic on a string.
Not recognizing when an array technique already learned (two pointers, sliding window, prefix sum) applies directly to a string problem.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why do array techniques like two pointers and sliding window apply directly to strings?
questions.answer: Because a string is an indexable, iterable sequence just like an array — the only difference is the element type is char instead of int.

questions.question: How would you check if a string is a palindrome in O(n) time and O(1) space?
questions.answer: Use two pointers starting at both ends, comparing characters and moving inward; if any pair disagrees, it's not a palindrome.

questions.question: How would you check if two strings are anagrams of each other?
questions.answer: Compare their lengths first, then use a frequency count (e.g. unordered_map<char, int>) — incrementing counts for one string and decrementing for the other; if every count ends at zero, they're anagrams.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: For DSA purposes, a string behaves just like an array of characters — indexable and iterable — so techniques like Two Pointers, Sliding Window, and frequency counting with unordered_map all apply directly. A palindrome check uses two pointers from both ends moving inward, comparing characters, in O(n) time and O(1) space. An anagram check uses frequency counting: tally each character in the first string, subtract for the second, and confirm every count cancels to exactly zero — the same pattern unordered_map used for counting occurrences elsewhere.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Strings (DSA)
sections.text: String = an array of char for algorithm purposes. Palindrome = two pointers closing inward. Anagram = tally up, tally down, check everything cancels to zero.

sections.title: Reverse String
sections.problemStatement: Write a function that reverses a string in place, given as a character array s, using O(1) extra space.
hints: This lesson's palindrome check already uses two pointers from both ends, comparing inward — reversing is the same shape, but SWAPPING instead of comparing.
Every pair (left, right) needs to trade places exactly once — think about what happens if you swap all the way until the pointers meet.
No extra array is needed: the swap happens directly on s, in place.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a new reversed array by reading s backward, then copy it back over s.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers from opposite ends, swapping in place until they meet — no extra array at all.

sections.title: Valid Palindrome
sections.problemStatement: Given a string s, considering only alphanumeric characters and ignoring case, return true if it reads the same forward and backward.

examples.input: s = "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"

hints: This is this lesson's exact palindrome-check pattern, with one twist: punctuation, spaces, and case need to be ignored first.
Rather than building a cleaned copy, the two pointers can just SKIP over non-alphanumeric characters as they move inward.
Compare characters case-insensitively (tolower on both sides) instead of comparing them directly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build a cleaned (alphanumeric-only, lowercased) copy, then use this lesson's palindrome check on it.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers directly on the original string, skipping non-alphanumeric characters on the fly — no cleaned copy needed.

sections.title: Longest Common Prefix
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of strings strs, return the longest common prefix shared by all of them. Return "" if there is none.
hints: The common prefix can never be longer than the SHORTEST string in the array — that's an immediate upper bound.
Compare all strings one CHARACTER POSITION at a time, rather than one whole string at a time — the moment any string disagrees at a position, the prefix stops there.
You can shrink a running candidate prefix by repeatedly trimming it against each string in turn, or scan column-by-column — either way, stop the instant a mismatch appears.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Start with the first string as the candidate prefix, and repeatedly shrink it by comparing against each subsequent string in turn.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(S) average, where S is total characters, but repeated substr() allocations add overhead
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(m) for the shrinking prefix copy

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Scan column by column (character position by character position) across all strings simultaneously, stopping at the first mismatch or the shortest string's end.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(S), S = total characters, no repeated allocations
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra, excluding the returned prefix

sections.title: Valid Anagram
sections.problemStatement: Given strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s.
constraints: s and t consist of lowercase English letters
hints: This is exactly this lesson's isAnagram pattern — same length check, same tally-and-cancel idea.
Since the problem guarantees lowercase English letters only, the character range is small and fixed — is a full hash map really necessary?
A fixed-size array of 26 counters (indexed by c - 'a') can replace the unordered_map, avoiding hashing overhead entirely.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Sort both strings — two true anagrams become character-for-character identical once sorted.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for the two sorts
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra beyond the sort itself

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: This lesson's tally-up/tally-down pattern, using a fixed 26-size array instead of a hash map since the alphabet is known and small.

sections.title: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String
sections.problemStatement: Given haystack and needle, return the index of needle's first occurrence in haystack, or -1 if it doesn't occur.
hints: Try matching needle starting at every position in haystack, checking character by character.
The moment a character mismatches, that starting position has failed — move on to try the next starting position.
This is literally what recall find() from Header Files/std::string does internally — string has a built-in method for this exact search.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every starting position in haystack, comparing needle character by character.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: std::string's built-in find() implements this search internally, typically with far better constant factors than a hand-written loop (production string libraries often use algorithms like KMP or Boyer-Moore under the hood for O(n+m) worst-case behavior).
